title: Gauge-Field-Mediated Symmetry Breaking of Matters Under Electromagnetic Fields and Its Impact on Spin Dynamics
authors: Uiseok Jeong, Esmaeil Taghizadeh Sisakht, Angel Rubio, Carsten A. Ullrich, Kyeong-Whan Kim, Noejung Park
abstract: When a condensed-matter system is subjected to external electromagnetic fields, the gauge-invariant formulation of physical operators must explicitly incorporate the gauge-field contribution. However, in the context of spin-orbit coupling (SOC), this gauge-field term is often regarded as negligible or merely additive compared to the canonical SOC, which is typically localized near atomic cores. Here, we demonstrate that the symmetry breaking and consequent spin dynamics are governed by the gauge-field term, without which the spins remain symmetry-constrained. We perform real-time time-dependent density functional theory calculations to investigate spin-orbit dynamics, focusing on representative cases with mirror, glide, and screw-rotational symmetry. We demonstrate that when the gauge-field term in the time-dependent Hamiltonian perturbs the symmetry of the canonical term, a dynamical spin state gradually develops during the time evolution, beyond the symmetry-frozen states. We suggest that, for nonequilibrium spin-orbit dynamics, the gauge-invariant formulation of SOC is not only formally required but also quantitatively essential, even for a weak external field.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the gauge-field component of spin-orbit coupling is crucial for capturing the true spin dynamics in materials under external electric fields. It shows that neglecting this term leads to an incorrect 'symmetry-frozen' description of spins, whereas including it reveals essential symmetry-breaking effects and spin accumulation.
main_problem: Investigating whether the gauge-field contribution to the spin-orbit coupling (SOC) Hamiltonian is essential for describing symmetry breaking and spin dynamics under external electromagnetic fields.
main_result: The gauge-field term is the primary driver of symmetry breaking in the SOC Hamiltonian; without it, spins remain 'symmetry-frozen,' whereas its inclusion allows for the development of dynamical spin states.
method: Real-time time-dependent density functional theory (rt-TDDFT) using the Quantum ESPRESS and the Kleinman-Bylander scheme to incorporate gauge-field-dependent SOC into pseudopotentials.
model_or_system: The study examines a 2D Rashba-type square lattice model and real materials including the topological insulator Bi2Se3, the Dirac semimetal ZrSiS, and a 1D trigonal Se chain.
key_observables: Spin expectation values (Sx, Sy, Sz), spin texture, spin-projected band structures, and spin accumulation.
important_parameters: External electric field strengths (e.g., 0.5 mV/A and 1.0 mV/A), presence/absence of the gauge-field term, and various crystal symmetries (mirror, glide, and screw-rotational).
main_assumptions: The relativistic expansion of the electron Hamiltonian is considered up to the (v/c)^2 order.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates mirror planes and compares spin evolution with and without the gauge-field term; Figure 2 shows the crystal structure of ZrSiS; other figures show spin-resolved 3D band structures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of gauge-invariance in SOC, defines the decomposed Hamiltonian, presents rt-TDDFT methodology, applies the framework to specific models and real materials (Bi2Se3, ZrSiS), and concludes with the necessity of the gauge-field term for nonequilibrium dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Mirror-reflection properties of the canonical and gauge-field parts of the SOC Hamiltonian for a two- dimensional square lattice. (a) The yellow lines denote the mirror planes, specifically ˆ Mx and ˆ My, which are perpendic- ular to the y and x axes, respectively. When an external field is applied along the y direction, Aext(t) = (0, Aext(t), 0), ˆ My mirror is broken, and the gauge-field part of the SOC Hamilto- nian becomes asymmetric with respect to Mx. (b) Static spin- projected band structure in which the Sx value measured for each band eigenstate is denoted by color. (c) Time-dependent spin value Sx(t) obtained from the real-time time-dependent Schrodinger equation, either...

FIG. 2. Symmetry breaking induced by external fields and the consequent spin dynamics in Bi2Se3 and ZrSiS. (a, b) Top views and (c, d) side views of Bi2Se3 and ZrSiS, respectively. The mirror planes of each structure are indicated as yellow solid and dashed lines. (c, d) Time profile of Sx(t), obtained from rt-TDDFT for (c) Bi2Se3 and (d) ZrSiS.

FIG. 3. Screw rotational symmetry of a trigonal chiral wire and the symmetry-lowering external axial electric field(Ezˆz). (b) Real-time profile of spins obtained with ˆHCN SOC, (c) The same real-time profiles computed with ˆHCN+GF SOC .

FIG. 4. Influence of ˆHGF SOC on a low-symmetry system for which the gauge-field term does not further break the sym- metry. (a) Schematic illustration of Bi2Se3 subjected to a 3% strain along the y direction, which breaks two mirror sym- metries while preserving only ˆ My. (b) Calculated x-direction spin (Sx) under the effect of electric bias applied along the y direction. (c) Se trigonal chain with 3% strain along the x-direction, removing the screw-rotation symmetry. (d) Cal- culated z-direction spin (Sz) of the strained Se chain in (c) under an electric bias applied along the z direction. In (b) and (d), the red solid lines correspond to the results obtained with the SOC term including...

FIG. 1. Spin-resolved three-dimensional band structure of the two-dimensional Rashba square-lattice model defined in Eq. (4) of the main text. The energy dispersion E(k) is plotted over the full two-dimensional Brillouin zone, with the color representing the expectation value of each spin component: (left) Sx, (center) Sy, and (right) Sz, in units of ℏ.
